coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from corn stalk shell by hydrothermal approach in near-critical water and applications in detecting and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhiyong
Wang, Qinghui
Zhou, Zhengxin
Zhao, Shengnan
Zhong, Shuangling
Xu, Lifeng
Gao, Yan
Cui, Xuejun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have diverse outstanding properties and potential application, but there are not many reports on the preparation of CQDs from agricultural waste without any addition of chemical reagent. Herein, a facile, economical and environmental route was developed for green synthesis of CQDs from corn stalk shell by hydrothermal approach in near-critical water. It’s worth noting that this method converted the biomass, which is regarded as agricultural wastes, into the carbon nano-materials with brilliant potential. The prepared CQDs present the great fluorescence property, the remarkable stability and biocompatibility, demonstrating this method is appreciate to produce the CQDs. The as-prepared CQDs can be employed in detecting and bioimaging domain, displaying distinctive potential for application. The superiorities of low-cost, renewability and sustainability drive the synthesis and applications of CQDs derived from biomass.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corn stalk
Near-critical water
Carbon quantum dots
Detecting
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HR-TEM image of the GQDs). (b) Size distribution.

(a) The photograph and fluorescence image of the CQD aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) XRD pattern of CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of CQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of CQDs (d) C 1 s, (e) O 1 s, (f) N 1 s.

(a) UV–vis absorption (Abs), PL and excitation (PLE) spectra of CQD aqueous solution. (b) PL emission spectra excited by various incident lights progressively from 300 to 390 nm with a 10 nm increment. (c) Lifetime decay of CQDs. (d) PL intensity of CQD aqueous solution at different NaCl concentration under 365 nm. (e) PL intensity of CQD aqueous solution at different pH values under 365 nm. (f) The PL intensity of CQDs for various times under 365 nm.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs with different concentration of ARS in the range of 0–150 μmol/L (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150 μmol/L) under 365 nm. (b) The relationship between F0/F and the concentration of ARS in the range of 0–150 μmol/L. (c) UV–visible absorption of ARS (red line), PL spectra of CQDs (blue line).

(a) Cell viability (%) estimated by MTT proliferation tests versus incubation concentrations of CQDs (0, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1). (b) Confocal fluorescence image at 405 nm excitation. (c) bright field image. (d) merged image.
The synthesis method of CQDs from corn stalk shell.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from corn stalk shell by hydrothermal approach in near-critical water and applications in detecting and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have diverse outstanding properties and potential application, but there are not many reports on the preparation of CQDs from agricultural waste without any addition of chemical reagent. Herein, a facile, economical and environmental route was developed for green synthesis of CQDs from corn stalk shell by hydrothermal approach in near-critical water. It’s worth noting that this method converted the biomass, which is regarded as agricultural wastes, into the carbon nano-materials with brilliant potential. The prepared CQDs present the great fluorescence property, the remarkable stability and biocompatibility, demonstrating this method is appreciate to produce the CQDs. The as-prepared CQDs can be employed in detecting and bioimaging domain, displaying distinctive potential for application. The superiorities of low-cost, renewability and sustainability drive the synthesis and applications of CQDs derived from biomass.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing of the global population results in a sharp increase in the demand for food, which gives rise to the accumulation of the agricultural wastes. As we all know, the agricultural wastes is rich in lignocelluloses [1], however, thanks to hard structure and the low nutritional value of the lignocelluloses, the application of agriculture waste has been limited, most of which are burned, buried and fed to the animals, leading to the pollution of the environment and the waste of biological resources. In recent years, due to the rapid shortage of non-renewable resources, the agricultural waste containing a large amount of lignocelluloses has become the research focus of scholars, and can be further refined to produce industrial products as a new carbon source [2]. Meanwhile, the agriculture waste has been explored and applied in many other fields. The most common methods to make use of agriculture waste are to prepare carbon-based materials [3], including porous carbon [4–7] and carbon quantum dots [1,8–10]. These carbon-based materials have extensive application in the fields of energy store [3], adsorption [11,12], catalyst support [13,14], capacitor [4,5], optical devices [15–17], bioimaging [18–20] and biosensing [21–24]. In this work, we emphasize the research of corn stalk. Compared with the other agriculture wastes, the yield of corn stalk is much higher than others. The corn stalk, the main crop residues, is one of the most abundantly accessible and reproducible resource. Besides, the corn stalk consists of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin, each of which can be the raw materials of serviceable chemicals. Here we do some research about degrading the corn stalk shell into the high value-added products.

Generally, the temperature between 150 °C and 374 °C is defined as near-critical water (NCW) [25] whose pressure is 0.4–22.1 MPa. NCW is a new media and great solvent developed under the concept of green chemistry. As the temperature rises, the polarity of water drops sharply, which has attracted widespread attention from many people. Besides, It is worth noting that the dielectric constant of the water drop below 30 when the temperature is increased to 250 °C, while the dielectric constants of methanol and acetonitrile are higher than 30, which indicates that the polarity of the water at 250 °C lower than that of methanol and acetonitrile [26,27]. Thus, the NCW can dissolve many organic compounds that are not environmental-friendly. The other superiorities of the NCW like low surface tension, low viscosity and low cohesive energy density are beneficial to decompose some insoluble compound [26]. What’s more, the water under the near-critical state has the brilliant mass transfer performance and higher ionization constant [25,28]. NCW can serve as an acidic catalyst [25,28] due to the combination of H+, while it can also be alkaline catalyst due to the hydroxide anions from the ionization of water. In addition, the most favorable advantages of NCW are toxic-free, low-cost, available and no harmful to the environment contrasting other organic reagents.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new 0-dimensional carbon-nanomaterials. Resulting from the outstanding quantum confinement effect [8], the CQDs show novel properties that have not found in other nano-materials, possessing the extensive and novel application value in the domain of photo-electricity [29,30]. Besides, owing to hypotoxicity, good solubility, chemical durability and remarkable photoluminescence of the CQDs, they can be extensively used in the field of bioimaging [2,19,31–33], biosensing [23,34–38] and photo-electric device [39,40]. According to the previously reported works, many approaches of preparing the CQDs have been proposed, which can be approximately divided into “top-down” methods and “bottom-up” methods. The “top-down” methods involve decomposing the carbon materials [41–43] (e.g. carbon black, graphene carbon nano-tube) by some physical and chemical processing methods. While these methods are widely employed for high yields, but these methods exhibit some inferiorities, limiting the application of CQDs. And the raw materials used in these approaches are not cheap and accessible. On the other hand, the CQDs can be prepared by the “bottom-up” methods which refer to assembling the organic molecule [32,44] (e.g. glucose, citric acid) into the CQDs with controllable size and uniform shape. Additionally, some methods of synthesizing CQDs from organic reagents do not conform to the idea of green chemistry. The employment of the toxic reagents can deteriorate the environment, out of the concept of green chemistry and sustainable. However, many concepts of using agricultural by-products as carbon sources to prepare high value-added carbon materials have attracted great attention from chemists, because the biomass is available and renewable, in line with the concept of green chemistry and sustainable development.

Here, we report an approach employing the corn stalk shell as the carbon source of fabricating the CQDs in the NCW. The preparation of CQDs is based on the fact that the lignocellulose can be partially dissolved in the NCW. In the synthesis of procedures (Scheme 1 ), the NCW acting as the organic reagents can dissolve some organic compounds, owing to the decreased polarity of the NCW at high temperature and high pressure. Compared to the multi-step organic synthesis steps, water phase reaction is simpler, more environmental-friendly and lower in cost. Moreover, the CQDs prepared in the NCW have excellent optical property and great stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from corn stalk shell
ce:section.ce:para: A sample of 20 g corn stalk shell was placed into the reaction kettle with 500 mL deionized (DI) water. Then the reaction starts at 270 °C and the pressure of 5 MPa for 10 min. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtrated with suction flask for obtaining the liquid. The liquid was filtrated through 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the insoluble impurities. Then the solution of CQDs was dialyzed by dialysis bag of 1000 Da to get rid of small molecules for 24 h. The solution dialyzed was freeze-dried into powdery solid of CQDs. The CQDs obtained was kept for further detection.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image were observed by JEM-2100F. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns was obtained with a Empyrean using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with an IRAffinity-1. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum was recorded on Lambda950 at room temperature, and fluorescence (PL) spectra was recorded by FLS920. X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB-250 photo-electron spectrometer using Al Kα radiation.

The quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was calculated by the following equation [45]:
ψ x
=
ψ s
I x
I s
A s
A x
η
x
2
η
s
2

where
ψ
is the fluorescence quantum yield; x and s represent the test substance and reference compound. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (
ψ s
= 0.54) was chosen as the reference. η is the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solution). A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. I is the integrated fluorescence intensity under the fluorescence emission spectrum.

For the detection of ARS, 100 μL 0.1 mg/mL CQDs solution and different volume of ARS (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 300 μL, 1 mmol/L) were added to a series of 2.0 mL calibrated test tubes. The solution was diluted to 2.0 mL by deionized water in tubes. Then the mixtures were mixed thoroughly for 10 min at room temperature before fluorescent measurement at 365 nm.

The cytotoxicity of cell was evaluated with the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay in A549 cells. A549 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with the concentration of 1 × 104 cells per well. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C with the atmosphere of 5% CO2. After being cultured for 24 h, the medium was replaced by fresh DMEM medium containing different concentrations of CQDs (0, 25, 50 and 100 mg/L). Cells cultured without CQDs were taken as the control. After incubation of 24 h and 48 h, respectively, 15 μL of a MTT solution (5.0 mg/ mL) was added to each well and the plates were further incubated for 4 h at 37 °C under the air containing 5% CO2. Then, the well supernatants were removed, and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well. The absorbance of the resultant the well contents was monitored at 490 nm using a microplate reader (Perlong Medical, DNM-9606). The relative cell viability was determined by comparing with the control. The experiments were repeated three times. The results are presented as mean ± standard deviation. The cell viability was calculated by following equation:
Cell viability (%) = (the absorbance of the experimental group/the absorbance of the control group) × 100%

A549 cells were cultured in DMEM culture medium with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin–streptomycin. Approximately 2 × 105 A549 cells were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 40 mm) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under an atmosphere of 5% CO2. Then CQDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 50 mg/L was introduced to the culture medium (2 mL per dish) with A549 cells. The cells were washed with PBS three times and examined under a confocal microscope (Zeiss, LSM 880 NLO) using lasers of 405 nm.

The CQDs were prepared by the degradation of corn stalk shell in the NCW. The obtained product was purified by the membrane, after the process of filtration. After dialysis treatment and freeze-drying treatment, the CQDs powder was obtained. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of the CQDs, from the TEM image, the size of CQDs is clearly observed. And the HR-TEM image reveals the detailed atomic resolution of the CQDs with a lattice parameter of 0.18 nm. Fig. 1b shows that the CQDs have the uniform size contribution ranging from 1.2 to 3.2 nm. The as-prepared water-soluble CQDs showed brown color in water in water (Fig. 2 a left), and the stability of the solution is perfect. The solution of the CQDs gives out blue fluorescence, when exposed to the UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 2a right) with a QY of 16% (Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 ( ψ = 0.54) as the reference). In order to confirm the CQDs, the obtained products are analyzed by a range of detection means, like XRD, FT-IR, XPS, TEM and HR-TEM. To illustrate the structure of CQDs, the XRD analysis was performed, as the Fig. 2b showing, a broad diffraction peak at 22.5° was observed, demonstrating that the structure of CQDs is disordered amorphous nanoparticles [46]. As shown from FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2c), a strong peak at ~ 1,609 cm−1 is ascribing to the C = C stretching vibration, and the peak at ~ 1,118 cm−1 is contributed to the deformation vibration of the C–H. Besides, peaks at ~ 3,355 cm−1, 1,707 cm−1, 1,517 cm−1 and 1205 cm−1 manifest the existence of O–H, C = O, C-N and C-O functional groups on the surface of the water-soluble CQDs. The functional groups of CQDs are further characterized by the XPS. The detail C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows strongly peak of C = C or C–C at 284.6 eV, remarkable C-O peak at 286.6.0 eV and prominent C = O peak at 288.4 eV. The digital O1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) demonstrates the presence of C = O and C-OH/C-O-C, whose peaks at 532 eV and 533 eV, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) shows the single peak at 398 eV, generated by the C-N-C, and the peak at 400 eV ascribed to N–H.

The degradation of the lignocellulose is still a serious problem for scientists, due to the complex structure and composition. Based on the previous work, we know that the processes of degrading lignocellulose consists of hydrolysis and polycondensation, after the degradation, the macromolecular polymer grow into the CQDs [1,2,47]. As we all know, natural biomass molecules are connected by the various chemical bonds such as hydrogen bonds [48], while, NCW provides the abundant power for breakage of the chemical bonds, giving birth to degrading nature biomass into oligomer and micro-molecules. Some substances with carboxyl groups contained in these micro-molecules, result in the solution acidic. In the processes of solution acidic and high temperature and high pressure, the amorphous state carbohydrate further degrades, generating more water-soluble small molecules [1]. All these small molecules extensively dissolve into the NCW, whose polarity is similar with the methanol and acetonitrile. Besides, in the process of high temperature and high pressure, these small molecules aggregate into polymer producing macromolecular compounds. Finally, these macromolecular compounds grow into the CQDs in the NCW. In this method, the NCW not only act as a scissor severing the macromolecular compounds into fragment, but also serve as the oxidizing agent and catalyst [28,49] availing for the preparation of CQDs.

The UV–visible spectrum (Fig. 3 a Abs) shows two excitonic absorption peak concentrated on 273 nm and 325 nm. The peak of 273 nm is attributed to
π → π
∗
electron transitions, and the peak of 325 nm is attributed to n
→ π
∗
electron transition [50,51]. And the Fig. 3a presents the remarkable emission peak (PL) (460 nm) excited at 365 nm (Fig. 3a PLE). In addition, as the photoluminescence spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows, the emission wavelength is dependent on the excited wavelength. The emission peaks gradually increase with the increasing of excited wavelength, suggesting that the emission wavelength could be changed by adjusting the excitation wavelength. As shown in the Fig. 3b, the emission wavelength is red shift, and the emission intensity gradually decreases, when the excitation wavelength increases. In term of excitation-dependent characterization of CQDs, it is a common property and can be applied to the bioimaging. The lifetime decay of CQDs (Fig. 3c) shows the double-exponential decay, and the lifetime for 14.44 ns. What’s more, The PL intensity stability of different concentrations of NaCl also is investigated under 365 nm (Fig. 3d). The result demonstrates that PL intensity is seldom floating, regardless of high or low concentration. The PL intensity stability is detected in a range of pH under 365 nm (Fig. 3e). Under the condition of acid to base, the PL intensity maintains outstanding stability without the affection of pH. The experiment results show that there are many –COOH on the surface of the CQDs, but the PL intensity of the CQDs hardly changes under alkaline conditions. The good stability of CQDs under different pH conditions is due to the fact that the PL intensity has nothing to do with the –COOH. Considering that the pH of physiological environment is 7 to 8, the PL intensity of this range is relatively stable, in favor of the application of biological field. The solution of as-prepared CQDs shows excellent stability and high dispersity. As shown in Fig. 3f, the stability of CQDs at different time was obtained. As time goes by, the PL intensity of CQDs hardly changes, so the stability of CQDs is great. Taking into the consideration that the PL intensity of CQDs at physiological environment and different time remain brilliant stability, the as-prepared CQDs shows noteworthy application potential.

Alizarin red S (ARS) is an organic dyestuff, extensively used in biological studies for calcium staining in tissue slices. It belongs to the anthraquinone salt, so the emission of ARS can bring about water pollution and the increase of concentration of ARS can result in the increase of poisonousness of water [52,53]. Fig. 4 a shows fluorescence spectra of CQDs/ARS system after adding the various concentration of ARS under 365 nm. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs gradually decreased in the process of increasing the concentration of ARS. As Fig. 4b showing, there is a great liner between CQDs and different concentration of ARS, F0/F (F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/ARS system in the absence and presence of ARS, respectively) gradually decreased with the decrease of concentration of ARS in the range of 10 ~ 150 μM. The linear regression equations is
F0/F = 0.8983 + 0.0085 [ARS].

The correlation regression coefficient (R2) is 0.9987. And the detection limit of ARS is 2.65 μM, which is determined by the equation LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals and s is the slope of the calibration curve. The decrease of PL intensity of CQDs results from the combination of CQDs and ARS. As shown in Fig. 4c, the UV spectra of ARS (red line) shows a broad absorbance peak in the range of 390 nm to 460 nm, the PL spectrum of CQDs (blue line) exhibits an emission peak between 420 nm and 470 nm, the two peaks overlap. When the combination of CQDs and ARS was exposed to the excitation, the ARS absorbed the fluorescence of CQDs, which led to the decrease of PL intensity of CQDs. This point can be explained by the electron transfer. The CQDs have the conjugated structure, the ARS, an anthraquinones matter, also have the conjugated structure. The electron transfer happening in the combination of CQDs and ARS generates that the emission of CQDs was absorbed by ARS, causing the quenching of the fluorescence.

The prepared CQDs shows great optical properties, as shown in Fig. 3, so the as-prepared CQDs are expected to apply to bioimaging. To determine whether the CQDs could be used as bioprobes, the toxicity and cell viability of the A549 cell were test after treatment with CQDs at various concentration. The result shown in Fig. 5 a demonstrates that whether at low concentration (25 and 50 mg/L) or high concentration (100 mg/L), CQDs is almost innocuous after being incubated for 24 h, because the cell viability was still more than 90%. Prolonging the time of incubation, the cell viability is still over 80% at the concentration of 25 and 50 mg/L. The cell viability is more than 75% when the cells are incubated for 48 h at the concentration of 100 mg/L of CQDs. These results suggest that the as-prepared CQDs have remarkable biocompatibility, can be applied to biological applications. Moreover, the in vitro bioimaging of A549 cells containing 50 mg/L of CQDs were investigated using confocal (Fig. 5). The cell incubated with CQDs exhibits strong blue fluorescence on excitation at 405 nm. Observing the Fig. 5b, the CQDs were internalized by the A549 cell rather than sticking on the surface of the cell, The pathway of the internalization is determined by z-axis imaging of the cells (Supplementary Fig. S1) [19]. Accordingly, the as-prepared CQDs with good biocompatibility and biodegradability [54] could be applied in the field of bioimaging.

We developed a method for fabricating the CQDs with the corn stalk shell at the appropriate situation. As a low-cost and accessible resource, the corn stalk shells produce the CQDs after being treated by NCW pyrolysis. The as-synthesized CQDs is stable and independent on pH and NaCl. Besides, CQDs can be applied to efficiently detect ARS with the characters of simplicity, sensitivity and low detection limit of 2.65 μM. Furthermore, we verified that the CQDs with remarkable biocompatibility have excellent application in the domain of bioimaging. Our research emphasizes that the approach of producing CQDs is green, simple and low-cost, and can realize the comprehensive utilization of biomass.

Zhiyong Li: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Qinghui Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Zhengxin Zhou: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Shengnan Zhao: Software, Validation. Shuangling Zhong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Lifeng Xu: Supervision. Yan Gao: Supervision. Xuejun Cui: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Program for Jilin Science and Technology Department (20200702055NC) and Independent Research Project in 2020 of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials (CXKT202008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106250.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
